summary: Gruber argues that Apple's decision to change Section 3.3.1 of the iPhone Developer Program License Agreement — banning cross-platform compilation tools like Flash CS5 and MonoTouch — is a rational competitive move to prevent meta-platforms from establishing themselves on top of Cocoa Touch. He frames the mobile era as a land grab where Apple wants its APIs and App Store to become the de facto standard, and allowing cross-platform toolkits would erode that lock-in advantage. He systematically analyzes how the change affects each stakeholder, concluding that iPhone users benefit because cross-platform toolkits have never produced quality native apps on Apple platforms. He contrasts the native iPhone Kindle app (excellent) with the cross-platform Mac Kindle app (a turd) as evidence.
key_points: Apple's goal is to establish Cocoa Touch and the App Store as the de facto standard mobile platform, not to achieve Windows-like market share but to own the quality tier

Flash CS5 and MonoTouch are meta-platforms, not cross-platform tools — Adobe's goal is to make developers write Flash apps that happen to run on iPhone, which directly threatens Apple's platform control
If a third-party meta-platform became dominant, Apple would lose control over when developers could adopt new iPhone OS features
Cross-platform toolkits have never produced top-notch native apps for any Apple platform — the Kindle app comparison illustrates this clearly
The web remains completely open on iPhone because no single company controls it, unlike Flash or .NET
money_quotes: Apple doesn't want everywhere, they just want everywhere good.
Flash CS5 and MonoTouch aren't so much cross-platform as meta-platforms.
Adobe's goal isn't to help developers write iPhone apps. Adobe's goal is to encourage developers to write Flash apps that run on the iPhone (and elsewhere) instead of writing iPhone-specific apps.
I'm not saying you have to like this. I'm not arguing that it's anything other than ruthless competitiveness.
Cross-platform software toolkits have never — ever — produced top-notch native apps for Apple platforms.

If you are constitutionally opposed to developing for a platform where you're expected to follow the advice of the platform vendor, the iPhone OS is not the platform for you. It never was. It never will be.
The Mac Kindle app is a turd that doesn't look, feel, or behave like a real Mac app.
key_insight: Apple banned cross-platform compilation tools not out of spite but to prevent meta-platforms from commoditizing Cocoa Touch, because whoever controls the abstraction layer controls the platform.
